title: Lab 2 · Analysis of Experimental Data
summary: The heaviest lab and the **8-point** Data-Analysis exam question. You run the full pipeline a metrologist applies to a batch of measurements: clean it (outliers), characterize it (mean, spread, distribution shape), test it (normality, hypotheses on the mean, ANOVA on operators), and model it (regression). Every step is a real Excel computation you do here by typing formulas and pressing *Check*. The capstone is **Chauvenet outlier identification**, exactly as graded in the exam.
objectives: Compute the sample mean and sample standard deviation (STDEV.S) of a measurement set.
Identify outliers with Chauvenet's criterion: α = 1/(2n), z = NORM.S.INV(1−1/(4n)), limits x̄ ∓ z·s.
Identify outliers with the IQR / box-plot rule (QUARTILE, 1.5·IQR fences).
Build a frequency distribution and test normality with a χ² goodness-of-fit (NORM.DIST, CHISQ.INV).
Read a normal probability plot via plotting positions (i−0.5)/n and NORM.S.INV.
Run a one-sample t-test on a mean (T.INV.2T) and a one-way ANOVA F-test (F.INV.RT / F.DIST.RT).
Fit a least-squares regression line and interpret slope, intercept and residuals.

blocks.text: The toolbox: which function for which step

In the exam you have Excel and a strict time budget. The whole Data-Analysis question is a chain of the functions below — learn their argument order cold. Italian Excel uses `;` as the argument separator and `,` as the decimal; this engine accepts both `;` and `,` as separators, so the formulas you type here transfer directly.

**STDEV.S** divides by $n-1$ and estimates $\sigma$ from a *sample* — always use it for experimental data. STDEV.P divides by $n$ (whole population only). VAR.S / VAR.P are their squared versions used in ANOVA.
The $q$-th quartile: $q=1$ gives $Q_1$ (25th percentile), $q=2$ the median, $q=3$ gives $Q_3$ (75th percentile). The interquartile range is $IQR=Q_3-Q_1$.

Inverse normal (quantile) functions. **NORM.S.INV(p)** returns the *standardized* value $z$ with cumulative probability $p$ (mean 0, st. dev. 1). **NORM.INV(p; mean; s)** returns the actual value on a normal with that mean and st. dev. They are linked by $x=\text{mean}+z\cdot s$.

With **cumulative = 1 (TRUE)** it returns $\Phi(x)$, the probability of being $\le x$ — used to get the theoretical count in a class as $n[\Phi(to)-\Phi(from)]$. With cumulative = 0 it returns the density (height of the bell), used only for plotting.

blocks.term: CHISQ.INV(p; ν) · CHISQ.INV.RT(α; ν) · CHISQ.DIST.RT(χ²; ν)
blocks.text: $\chi^2$ tools. **CHISQ.INV.RT(α; ν)** is the right-tail critical value (use for a one-sided goodness-of-fit). **CHISQ.INV(p; ν)** is the left-tail quantile (use both tails for a two-sided acceptance band). **CHISQ.DIST.RT(χ²; ν)** turns a statistic into its right-tail p-value. $\nu$ = degrees of freedom.

Student-$t$ tools for a two-sided mean test. **T.INV.2T(α; ν)** returns the positive critical value leaving $\alpha$ split over both tails (e.g. $\alpha=0.05,\ \nu=19\Rightarrow2.093$). **T.DIST.2T(|t|; ν)** returns the two-tailed p-value.

blocks.term: F.INV.RT(α; ν₁; ν₂) · F.DIST.RT(F; ν₁; ν₂)
blocks.text: Fisher tools for ANOVA. **F.INV.RT(α; ν₁; ν₂)** is the right-tail critical $F$ (between-dof $\nu_1$, within-dof $\nu_2$). **F.DIST.RT(F; ν₁; ν₂)** is the right-tail p-value. Note F.INV.RT$(\alpha,\dots)=$ F.INV$(1-\alpha,\dots)$.

blocks.title: The pipeline order
blocks.text: **1.** mean & st. dev. → **2.** remove outliers (Chauvenet / IQR) and recompute → **3.** frequency distribution + $\chi^2$ normality → **4.** normal probability plot → **5.** hypothesis test on the mean → **6.** ANOVA for an operator effect → **7.** regression for a drift. Each stage reuses the cleaned mean and st. dev. of the stage before.
The complete worked example (Es01)

Before drilling each stage in isolation, walk the **actual lab dataset** end to end. In Es01, five operators each measured the same part **ten** times, giving **50 readings** (in mm). The four exercises below run *that* dataset through the first half of the pipeline — raw statistics → Chauvenet → recompute → frequency classes — and every answer reproduces the corresponding cell of the official solution workbook. Do these first to see the method on real numbers; the stage-by-stage exercises that follow generalize it.

blocks.title: The Es01 numbers to remember
blocks.text: Raw 50 readings: $\bar x=4.506192$ mm, $s=0.0029867$ mm, min $=4.4988$, max $=4.5139$ mm. Chauvenet ($P_{xL}=1/(4n)=0.005$) gives limits $[4.498499,\,4.513885]$ mm — the **maximum 4.5139 mm is the single outlier** (it exceeds the upper limit), while the minimum 4.4988 mm is borderline but stays inside. After replacing the outlier, $\bar x=4.5061823$ mm and $s$ drops to $0.0025677$ mm; the frequency field $\bar x\pm4s$ is split into $\sqrt{50}+4\approx11$ classes of width $0.0018674$ mm.

blocks.title: Es01 · Step 1 — descriptive statistics of the raw data
blocks.caption: The real 50-reading dataset: count, mean, sample st. dev., min/max, quartiles.

blocks.title: Es01 · Step 2 — Chauvenet outlier check
blocks.caption: Limits via NORM.INV; the maximum 4.5139 mm is excluded, matching the Escl. sheet.

blocks.title: Es01 · Step 3 — recompute after outlier management
blocks.caption: Cleaned mean and st. dev. (the spread shrinks once the outlier is replaced).

blocks.title: Es01 · Step 4 — frequency-distribution set-up
blocks.caption: Examined field x̄ ± 4s, √n + 4 classes, class width — feeding the χ² test.

blocks.text: Stage 1 — Mean and standard deviation
Everything downstream is built on $\bar x$ and $s$. The single most common slip is using STDEV.P instead of STDEV.S.

blocks.title: Exercise 1 — mean and st. dev. of a sample

blocks.text: Stage 2 — Outlier management

Real measurement sets contain occasional *accidents* — a misread, a vibration, a chip. Before characterizing the process we must decide, objectively, whether a suspicious value should be discarded. Two criteria are in the syllabus.

A reading is rejected if the expected number of equally-or-more-extreme samples in $n$ trials is below $\tfrac12$. Per datum the acceptable risk is $\alpha=\dfrac{1}{2n}$; split over the two tails each gets $\dfrac{1}{4n}$. The standardized cut is $z=\text{NORM.S.INV}\!\left(1-\dfrac{1}{4n}\right)$ and the exclusion limits are $\bar x\mp z\,s$ (equivalently $\text{NORM.INV}\!\left(\tfrac{1}{4n},\bar x,s\right)$ and $\text{NORM.INV}\!\left(1-\tfrac{1}{4n},\bar x,s\right)$).

blocks.title: Exam capstone — read the units of every box
blocks.text: The graded Chauvenet question asks for **seven** numbers: mean, sample st. dev., the risk $\alpha$ (as a probability, e.g. 0.025 — *not* 2.5 %), the lower and upper exclusion limits, whether an outlier exists (Yes/No), and the outlier's value (0 if none). With $n=20$: $\bar x=44.90$, $s=3.01$, $\alpha=1/(2n)=0.025$, limits $38.16$ / $51.64$, outlier **Yes**, value **36**. Reporting $\alpha$ as a percentage, or using STDEV.P, loses marks.

blocks.title: Exercise 2 — Chauvenet's criterion (the 8-point exam question)
blocks.caption: Reproduces the real exam roughness dataset: 20 readings from 4 operators.

blocks.title: Exercise 3 — Chauvenet on a large set via NORM.INV

blocks.text: A distribution-free alternative: flag anything below $Q_1-1.5\,IQR$ or above $Q_3+1.5\,IQR$. Because quartiles ignore the tails, one wild point cannot inflate the fences the way it would inflate $s$ — so the IQR rule is *robust*. For a normal distribution the $1.5\,IQR$ fences sit at about $\pm2.7\sigma$.

blocks.title: After removing an outlier
blocks.text: Discard the flagged value, then **recompute** $n$, $\bar x$ and $s$ on the survivors before continuing. The official Chauvenet *limits*, however, are computed once from the raw sample.
Stage 3 — Frequency distribution and the χ² normality test

We bin the cleaned data into classes and compare the observed counts with what a normal distribution predicts. If they agree, we may treat the data as Gaussian (the basis for every later confidence interval).
The reference workbook spans the **examined field** $\bar x\pm4s$ and uses $\sqrt{n}+4$ classes; the class width is the field length divided by the number of classes.

blocks.caption: Observed vs expected counts; expected from the cumulative normal.

blocks.title: Degrees of freedom
blocks.text: $\nu=(\text{classes})-1-(\text{parameters estimated from the data})$. Because we estimate **both** $\bar x$ and $s$ from the sample, subtract $1+2=3$. Forgetting the $-2$ is the classic $\chi^2$ error and changes the critical value.

blocks.title: Exercise 6 — χ² goodness-of-fit to a normal

blocks.text: The reference lab runs the test **two-sided** at 80 % confidence: it accepts normality (and declares *no systematic effect*) when the experimental $\chi^2$ falls between a lower and an upper boundary. A $\chi^2$ that is too *small* is just as suspicious as one that is too large.
Stage 4 — Normal probability plot

A graphical normality check. Sort the data, give the $i$-th value the plotting position $p_i=\dfrac{i-0.5}{n}$, convert to a standard-normal quantile $z_i=\text{NORM.S.INV}(p_i)$, and plot $x_{(i)}$ against $z_i$. A straight line means normal; an S-shape signals skew or heavy tails. The fitted line has slope $\approx s$ and intercept $\approx\bar x$.
Stage 5 — Hypothesis test on the mean

Does the process mean match a nominal target $\mu_0$? Form the $t$-statistic, compare it with the two-sided Student-$t$ critical value (or read the p-value). With small samples the $t$ distribution — fatter-tailed than the normal — is the correct reference.

blocks.caption: One-sample t-statistic; the denominator s/√n is the standard error of the mean.

blocks.text: Stage 6 — ANOVA: is there an operator effect?

With several operators (columns), ANOVA compares the variance *between* column means with the variance *within* columns. If the between-variance dominates, the operator differences are systematic, not random scatter.

blocks.caption: m = rows per column. Between-dof = k−1, within-dof = N−k.

blocks.title: Reading the F-test
Exercise 11 — the same decision via the p-value

blocks.text: Stage 7 — Linear regression

If a drift is suspected (measurement value vs time/index), fit a least-squares line $x=a+b\,t$. The slope $b$ is the sensitivity / drift rate; the residuals (observed − predicted) reveal whether a straight line is adequate. There is no SLOPE function in this engine, so we build the least-squares estimate from the deviation sums — exactly the underlying formula.

blocks.tex: b=\frac{\sum (t_i-\bar t)(x_i-\bar x)}{\sum (t_i-\bar t)^2},\qquad a=\bar x-b\,\bar t
blocks.caption: Least-squares slope and intercept; the line always passes through (t̄, x̄).

blocks.title: Exercise 12 — least-squares slope, intercept, prediction, residual

blocks.text: Check your understanding
Quick conceptual checks on the traps that cost marks.

practice.prompt: Which Excel function gives the sample standard deviation that should be used for experimental data?
practice.explanation: A finite measurement set is a *sample* of the underlying process, so we estimate $\sigma$ with Bessel's correction: **STDEV.S**, dividing by $n-1$. **A** (STDEV.P) divides by $n$ and is only correct for a complete population — it underestimates the spread and is the classic exam trap. **B** is a variance, not a standard deviation, and also the population form. **D** is the mean absolute deviation, a different statistic the syllabus does not use here.
practice.theory: Sample statistics use $n-1$ degrees of freedom because one is spent estimating the mean.

practice.prompt: A χ² goodness-of-fit to a normal uses 11 classes, and both the mean and the standard deviation are estimated from the data. The degrees of freedom are
practice.explanation: $\nu = (\text{classes}) - 1 - (\text{parameters estimated}) = 11 - 1 - 2 = 8$. **A** forgets every subtraction. **B** subtracts only the $-1$ for the total-count constraint. **C** subtracts the $-1$ and one parameter, forgetting that *both* $\bar x$ and $s$ were fitted. Each parameter estimated from the same data removes one more degree of freedom and lowers the critical value.
practice.theory: Every constraint imposed on the expected counts (total count, each fitted parameter) costs one degree of freedom in a χ² test.

practice.prompt: In the two-sided 80 %-confidence χ² acceptance band, the experimental statistic is **9.12** and the boundaries are 3.49 and 13.36. The conclusion is

options.content: Do not reject normality — 9.12 lies inside [3.49, 13.36], no systematic effect
The test is invalid because χ² cannot be compared two-sided

practice.explanation: Since $3.49 < 9.12 < 13.36$, the statistic is inside the acceptance band, so the null hypothesis of a normal distribution is **not** rejected and no systematic effect is evidenced (at a 20 % first-kind risk). **A** and **C** misread the position relative to the band. **D** is wrong: a two-sided χ² band is exactly the workbook's method — a χ² far below the lower boundary would itself be suspicious (an unrealistically perfect fit).
practice.theory: A two-sided χ² band rejects both an implausibly bad fit (χ² too high) and an implausibly perfect one (χ² too low).

practice.prompt: In a one-way ANOVA the variance ratio is $F = 5.38$ and the critical value at 80 % confidence is $F_{crit} = 1.565$. What do you conclude about the operators?

options.content: $F > F_{crit}$, so the differences between operators are systematic (reject equal means)
$F > F_{crit}$, so the operators are equivalent
No conclusion is possible without the regression slope
$F < F_{crit}$, so there is no operator effect

practice.explanation: $F = MS_{between}/MS_{within}=5.38$ greatly exceeds $F_{crit}=1.565$: the between-operator variance dominates the random scatter, so we reject the null of equal means — the operator effect is **systematic** (with a 20 % first-kind risk). **B** has the right inequality but the wrong meaning. **D** has the inequality backwards ($5.38>1.565$). **C** is irrelevant — ANOVA needs no regression. Equivalently the p-value $\text{F.DIST.RT}(5.38;4;45)=0.0013<0.20$.
practice.theory: ANOVA declares an effect systematic when the signal (between-group MS) outweighs the noise (within-group MS) beyond the critical F.

practice.prompt: On a normal probability plot, the $i$-th ordered value is assigned the plotting position
practice.explanation: The standard plotting position is $p_i=\frac{i-0.5}{n}$ and the theoretical quantile is $z_i=\text{NORM.S.INV}(p_i)$. **A** ($i/n$) sends the largest value to $p=1$, where $z=+\infty$ — the $-0.5$ continuity correction exists precisely to avoid that. **C** confuses the plotting position with a cumulative probability of the data value. **D** leaves the rank unscaled, so it is not a probability. Plotting $x_{(i)}$ against $z_i$ yields a straight line when the data are normal.
practice.theory: The (i−0.5)/n correction keeps the extreme order statistics off the 0 and 1 boundaries where the normal quantile diverges.
